courses.description: This course is an introduction to the basic principles of management, strategic planning, quality assurance, health economics and financing, and health policy analysis that serves as a foundation to more advanced coursework in health policy & management. This course is benchmarked to meet certain part of the competencies of the Part A, Diplomate Membership level of the UK Public Health Examination, the US Certified Public Health Exam of the Association of Schools of Public Health as well as Part I Examination in Administrative Medicine of the HK College of Community Medicine.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For TPg student 
- Master of Public Health / Postgraduate Diploma in Public Health;- MSc/PgD in Epidemiology and Biostatistics; - MSc/PgD in Health Services Management;- MSc/PgD in Health Promotion and Behavioural Health Programme.
courses.learning_outcomes: The objective of this course is to acquire an understanding of the basic principles of healthcare system, policy, financing, and health economics. Students will become familiar with the basic management theories and concepts that underlie the operations of healthcare organizations. The course will familiarize students to structure, process and outcomes of health services and students will learn to apply systems thinking to resolve organizational issues.
courses.course_syllabus:
| Healthcare Policy and Management 1 |
| --- |
| Healthcare Policy and Management 2 |
| Healthcare Policy and Management 3 |
| Healthcare Policy and Management 4 |
| Health Economics & Healthcare Financing 1: Economics in Health Care (1) |
| Health Economics & Healthcare Financing 2: Economics in Health Care (2) |
| Health Economics & Healthcare Financing 3: Economic Evaluation |
| Health Economics & Healthcare Financing 4: Economic Evaluation |
| Healthcare Management & Organization 1: Healthcare Organizations |
| Healthcare Management & Organization 2 : Managing People in Health Care |
| Healthcare Management & Organization 3: Quality & Performance Management |
| Healthcare Management & Organization 4: Strategy & Planning |
courses.required_readings:
* Health Systems Policy, Finance and Organization, edited by Guy Carrin, Kent Buse, Harald Kristian heggenhougen, and Stella R. Quah. Elsevier, 2009.

Hong Kong's Health System : reflections, perspectives and visions by Leung GM and Bacon Shone J
courses.recommended_readings:
* Essentials of Health Services Stephen J Williams pub Delmar 2001
* Health Economics, by Frank A. Sloan and Chee-Ruey Hsieh, MIT Press 2012
* Healthcare Management – Organization Design and Behavior (5th edition) written by Stephen M Shortell and Arnold D. Kaluzny
* Images of Organization: Gareth Morgan

Management: Stephen P. Robbins (2nd Edition)

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Health Economics: Economic Evaluation

meetings.instructor: Professor YIP Hon Kei
Professor YIP Hon Kei

courses.description: There is a fundamental question asked in public health and health care in general: how can we get the best results, in terms of better health, from the resources which are available in the community? Health Economics, as a field, attempts to provide an explicit and rationale framework to improve the efficient and equitable allocation of health care resources. In this course, we will examine selected topics in health economics that have major implications for healthcare delivery, financing and clinical and public health research. Essential economic theories and methods for exploring each topic will be discussed, along with the role of markets and government. We will go through different models of healthcare financing from a policymaking perspective, applying measures such as equity and efficiency to evaluate them. In addition, basic skills of conducting economic evaluation, such as cost-effectiveness analysis, will also be introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health, MSc Health Services Management, PgD in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successfully completing this course, students will be able to:
1. describe and apply essential economic theories and methods relevant to the study of health care system and the improvement of public health
2. conduct basic economic evaluation in practice
3. describe how healthcare can be financed and resources can be allocated to achieve efficiency, equity, sustainability and responsiveness
courses.course_syllabus: There is a fundamental question asked in public health and health care in general: how can we get the best results, in terms of better health, from the resources which are available in the community? Health Economics, as a field, attempts to provide an explicit and rationale framework to improve the efficient and equitable allocation of health care resources. In this course, we will examine selected topics in health economics that have major implications for healthcare delivery, financing and clinical and public health research. Essential economic theories and methods for exploring each topic will be discussed, along with the role of markets and government. We will go through different models of healthcare financing from a policymaking perspective, applying measures such as equity and efficiency to evaluate them. In addition, basic skills of conducting economic evaluation, such as cost-effectiveness analysis, will also be introduced.

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Healthcare Financing

meetings.instructor:
Professor FUNG Hong, 
Professor WU Yushan

courses.description: This course introduces the key policy and economic principles that underpin the approaches to healthcare financing and the performance of health systems in relation to Universal Health Coverage.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health, MSc Health Services Management, PgD in Health Services Management
courses.course_syllabus:
| Introduction to healthcare financing and Universal Health Coverage | |
| --- | --- |
| |
| Healthcare financing models and health system performance | |
| |
| Mixed health systems, private health insurance, and payment systems | |
| |
| Health system evolution in Hong Kong and Singapore | |
| |
courses.required_readings: 1. Barbara McPake, Charles Normand, Samantha Smith. “Health Economics: An International Perspective” (Third Edition). Routledge, 2013.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. WHO Western Pacific Regional Office. Universal Health Coverage: Moving Towards Better Health – Action Framework for the Western Pacific Region, 2016.
2. WHO/World Bank. Health Systems for Universal Health Coverage – A Joint Vision for Healthy Lives, 2017.
3. Country references provided on Blackboard as references for the Group Presentation and Assignment.
4. Case study readings

- PgD Health Services Management

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Quality and Safety Management

meetings.instructor: Professor LAI Bo San Paul

courses.description:
This course introduces students to
(1)the concept, framework, practical approaches and current movement for safe, efficient, effective, and patient-centred healthcare;(2)principle and practices of incident management and patient relations;(3)concepts of errors in healthcare and principles and strategies in quality improvement
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health, Master of Science in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
1a. Understand the discipline of risk management, patient safety and patient relations
1b. Apply the concept and approach to
(i) identify and manage clinical risks,
(ii) manage adverse event,
(iii) enhance patient-engagement and relations, and
(iv) lead change (in quality and safety).

2a. Apply concepts of errors in healthcare improvements.
2b. Apply principles, tools, evaluation strategies and documentation method in quality improvement
courses.required_readings: Reading list will be announced to students before the course starts.
courses.recommended_readings: Reading list will be announced to students before the course starts.

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Medico-legal Management

meetings.instructor:
Professor HUNG Chi Tim, 
Professor LAI Bo San Paul

Professor HUNG Chi Tim,

courses.description: This course covers the principles and practices of medico-legal management in modern healthcare. The course uses case studies to cover important topics in medico-legal management including theory and principles of complaints handling and investigation, clinical negligence and responsibility, and risk management strategies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: for students in Master of Public Health, Master of Science in Health Services Management and Postgraduate diploma in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Learn the legal principles and issues in relation to medical treatment from birth to death
2. Learn the application of legal knowledge in handling complaints, clinical negligence, risk management and other aspects of health services management
courses.course_syllabus: This course covers the principles and practices of medico-legal management in modern healthcare. The course uses case studies to cover important topics in medico-legal management including theory and principles of complaints handling and investigation, clinical negligence and responsibility, and risk management strategies.

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Health Services Planning

meetings.instructor: Professor FUNG Hong

courses.description: This course aims to enrich students with the knowledge and acquire competencies in health services planning. The scope of the course ranges from planning for the health of the population to planning of physical facilities. Students will learn to appraise the planning issues of a region or hospital through a case study, group presentation and class discussion.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health, Master of Science in Health Services Management and Postgraduate diploma in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
Student should be able to:
1. Understand the principles in assessing the healthcare needs of a population
2. Plan the delivery system and capacity to meet the healthcare needs of a population
3. Apply the principles of physical planning to improve patient care and patient safety
4. Appraise the planning challenges of a healthcare facility
courses.course_syllabus: This course aims to enrich students with the knowledge and acquire competencies in health services planning. The scope of the course ranges from planning for the health of the population to planning of physical facilities. Students will learn to appraise the planning issues of a region or hospital through a case study, group presentation and class discussion.

meetings.instructor: Professor FUNG Hong

courses.description: This course introduces students to the principles and practices of today’s corporate communication and marketing in healthcare. Examples and case studies, from healthcare where appropriate, are used to illustrate the up-to-date treatment of the key aspects of corporate communication and marketing.

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Advanced Organization Management

meetings.instructor: Professor HUNG Chi Tim

courses.description: This course aims to equip students with a more in-depth knowledge in organizational management by going into areas of organizational health and organizational culture. Organizational health and organizational culture are important and related concepts affecting the performance of the organization with respect to external and internal stakeholders. The course emphasizes on learning through reflection and practical application of key concepts in organizational health and culture supported by case studies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health, Master of Science in Health Services Management and Postgraduate diploma in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the broad base of knowledge and key concepts in organizational health and organizational culture.
2. Apply the knowledge and learning to appraise the problems the student faces in day-to-day management and operations in healthcare organizations

courses.title: Driving Healthcare Improvement with Data

meetings.instructor:
Dr. LEUNG Yee Man, 
Professor HUNG Chi Tim

courses.description:
This course introduces students to
(1) The concept and practice of data-driven healthcare improvement in the context of interprofessional teamwork;(2) Data visualization and analytic tools that inform decision making in the context of clinical practices (for example, run charts and control charts);(3) Data and change management: Feedback and improving the whole process
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Public Health and MSc in Health Services Management
courses.learning_outcomes:
Student should be able to:
1. Understand the value of data management in healthcare management

2. Apply the concept and approach of data management to
(i) identify and manage clinical and non-clinical care in the healthcare environment, and to
(ii) improve healthcare services using the appropriate tool

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Complaints Management in Healthcare

meetings.instructor: Ms. WONG Yuen Fong Pauline
Ms. WONG Yuen Fong Pauline

courses.description:
This course aims to equip students with a macro concept as well as in-depth knowledge and skills to meet the challenges of complaints against healthcare services providers. It introduces students to:
1. the complaints systems in public and private healthcare services institutions in Hong Kong; 2. the knowledge, strategy and practical skills in complaints management including mediation and conciliation;3. the legal requirements of complaints mechanisms and compliance issues;4. the law relevant to complaints management including the Apology Ordinance and the Mediation Ordinance;5. the application of complaints management skills and legal principles in resolving disputes.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Health Services Management and Master of Public Health
courses.required_readings:
1. Hospital Authority’s 2-Tier Complaint Handling System. Available at:

* (https://www.ha.org.hk/visitor/ha\_visitor\_text\_index.asp?Content\_ID=261655&Lang=ENG&Ver=TEXT)*

1. *Chapter 7 (Complaints Management), Code of Practice for Private Hospitals*, issued by the Director of Health under section 102 of the Private Healthcare Facilities Ordinance (Cap. 633) in June 2019
2. Apology Ordinance (Cap. 631)
3. Mediation Ordinance (Cap. 620)
4. Private Healthcare Facilities Ordinance (Cap. 633)
5. *Practice Direction 30 (Mediation) & Practice Direction 18.1 (The Personal Injuries List)* made under the Rules of the High Court. Available at: <https://legalref.judiciary.hk/lrs/common/pd/pdcontent.jsp?pdn=PD18.1.htm&lang=EN>
6. Labour Department of Hong Kong, *Conciliation Service and Claims*. Available at:

<https://www.labour.gov.hk/eng/public/csc.htm>

<https://www.lr.labour.gov.hk/web/en/services/services.html>

1. Equal Opportunities Commission (2019, *Work Progress and Key Focuses of Equal Opportunities Commission*, LegCo Paper (CB(2)1427/18-19(05)). Available at:

<https://www.legco.gov.hk/yr18-19/english/panels/ca/papers/ca20190520cb2-1427-5-e.pdf>

Also see:

<https://www.eoc.org.hk/en/enquiries-and-complaints/what-is-conciliation>

<https://www.legco.gov.hk/yr18-19/english/panels/ca/papers/ca20190520cb2-1427-5-e.pdf>

1. Raymond Hai Ming Leung, Hong Kong Mediation Handbook, 2nd ed.
2. Nadja Alexander, The Hong Kong Mediation Manual, 2nd ed.
3. The Efficiency Unit, HKSAR, *A Guide to Handling Complaint and Public Enquiries* (Aug 2009)
4. The Ombudsman of Hong Kong, *Annual Report 2020*
5. Hospital Authority, *HA Annual Appreciation, Feedback & Complaints Management 2020-2021*
6. Jonathan Reinarz and Rebecca, *Complaints, Controversies and Grievances in Medicine – Historical & Social Science perspectives*
7. Two sides of every story: the perspectives of complainants and doctors, Law and Policy, 16, 2, 148-83
8. Friele RD, Sluijs EM. Patient expectations of fair complaint handling in hospitals: empirical data. BMC Health Serv Res 2006;6.
9. Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO). My expectations for raising concerns and complaints, 2014. Available at: https://www.ombudsman.org.uk/sites/default/ files/Report\_My\_expectations\_for\_raising\_concerns\_and\_ complaints.pdf
10. Vexatious litigants and unusually persistent complainants and petitioners: from querulous paranoia to querulous behaviour. Behavioral Sciences and the Law, Behav. Sci. Law 24: 333–349 (2006), Published online 16 May 2006 in Wiley InterScience (www.interscience.wiley.com). DOI: 10.1002/bsl.671
11. Friele RD, Reitsma PM, de Jong JD. Complaint handling in healthcare: expectation gaps between physicians and the public; results of a survey study. BMC Res Notes 2015;8.
12. Wessel M, Lynøe N, Juth N, et al. The tip of an iceberg? A cross-sectional study of the general public's experiences of reporting healthcare complaints in Stockholm, Sweden. BMJ Open 2012;2:e000489.
13. Bourne T, Wynants L, Peters M, et al. The impact of complaints procedures on the welfare, health and clinical practise of 7926 doctors in the UK: a cross-sectional survey. BMJ Open 2015;5:e006687.
14. Cunningham W. The immediate and long-term impact on new Zealand doctors who receive patient complaints. N Z Med J Online 2004;117.
23. Care and Quality Commission (CQC). Complaints matter, 2014. Available at: https://www.cqc.org.uk/sites/default/files/ 20141208\_complaints\_matter\_report.pdf

courses.subject: HSYS
courses.title: Digital Transformation: Strategic Leadership in the Age of AI

meetings.instructor:
Dr. LEUNG Yee Man, 
Dr. YAU Zi May Susan

courses.description: This course equips health leaders tools to drive AI transformation with strategies balancing innovation and humanism.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students in Master of Public Health and students in MSc Health Services Management and
 students in MSc Epidemiology and Biostatistics
courses.learning_outcomes:
The exponential growth of artificial intelligence (AI) and other digital technologies is reshaping healthcare at an unprecedented pace. While these advancements promise improved diagnostics, personalized treatments, and operational efficiencies, they also create unique challenges for healthcare leaders. Chief among these challenges is managing the human side of change—ensuring that healthcare professionals, patients, and organizations adapt to this transformative era. This course addresses the critical knowledge gap faced by healthcare leaders: how to strategically navigate the transition from the more conventional data-driven decision-making to the new AI-enhanced systems while fostering trust, collaboration, and ethical implementation. Participants will explore the evolution from Business Intelligence (BI) to Artificial Intelligence (AI), understand the implications of AI on clinical workflows and workforce dynamics, and develop actionable strategies to lead their organizations through this complex transformation. By focusing on both the technological opportunities and the human-centred challenges of AI adoption, this program equips healthcare professionals with the tools they need to drive innovation, manage change, and deliver better patient outcomes in a rapidly evolving landscape.

Detailed Course Objectives
Strategic Transformation Objectives
1. Understand the historical evolution of digital transformation in healthcare, from data-centric systems (BI) to algorithm-driven innovations (AI).
2. Analyze how AI is transforming clinical workflows, decision-making processes, and patient care delivery.
3. Equip participants with frameworks to assess their organization's readiness for AI integration and digital transformation.
Technological Competency Objectives
1. Explore key AI applications in healthcare, including predictive analytics, diagnostic tools, personalized medicine, and operational automation.
2. Learn how to evaluate emerging technologies for their potential impact on clinical and administrative processes.
3. Understand the limitations of AI systems in healthcare, including data quality issues, algorithmic biases, and regulatory constraints.
Leadership and Change Management Objectives
1. Develop leadership strategies for managing resistance to AI adoption among healthcare professionals.
2. Foster a culture of innovation by addressing workforce concerns about job security and evolving roles.
3. Build trust in AI systems through transparent communication, ethical practices, and collaborative implementation.
Human-Centred Implementation Objectives
1. Address the unique burden of managing the human side of change in an era of rapid technological advancement.
2. Design strategies for upskilling healthcare professionals to work effectively alongside AI systems.
3. Create governance structures that ensure ethical use of AI technologies while maintaining patient-centric care.
Practical Application Objectives
1. Develop actionable digital transformation plans tailored to specific healthcare settings.
2. Anticipate future trends in AI and emerging technologies that will shape the future of healthcare delivery.
3. Build adaptive strategies to ensure long-term sustainability of digital innovations within organizations.
This course provides a balanced focus on technological innovation and human-centred leadership, ensuring that participants are equipped not only to adopt cutting-edge tools but also to lead their teams through the cultural and operational changes required for successful digital transformation in healthcare.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Digital Transformation Foundations
2. Digital Transformation Strategies and their Implementations
3. Leadership and Organizational Change Management
4. Digital Governance and Responsible AI
courses.required_readings: Reading list will be provided before the class begins.
courses.recommended_readings: Reading list will be provided before the class begins.
